- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 48, 0, 377067, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 48, 0, 377421, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4dc9-8577-71cc-8b72-6da8cc9f484e', conversation_id='019f4dc9-8577-71cc-8b72-6da7819bd6fc')

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('es', 'spanish', 'spanisch') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('it', 'italian', 'italienisch') THEN 'IT'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",

WITH cleaned_amounts AS (
    SELECT
        *,
        CASE
            WHEN amount IS NULL OR TRIM(amount) = '' THEN NULL
            ELSE
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(amount), E'^[\\$€£]+', '', 'g'),
                        E'^(?:Euro|Dollar|EUR|USD|GBP|CHF)\\s+', '', 'gi'
                    ),
                    E'[\\s]', ''
                )
        END AS raw_amount
    FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
)
SELECT
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preisanfrage') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYY-MM-DD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '0' THEN 0.0
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$' THEN
            (REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION * 10 +
            (SPLIT_PART(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'[.]', '', 'g'), ',', -1))::DOUBLE PRECISION / 10
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN
            (raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE
            CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', '$', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM cleaned_amounts

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='I can see several issues in the files I wrote:

1. **Account.sql** has a syntax error: `WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('gold',) THEN 'Gold'` - there's a trailing comma inside the IN clause which is invalid PostgreSQL. It should be `IN ('gold')` or just `'gold'`. Also, I have duplicate entries for SILBER/Silver in both Bronze and Silver cases.

2. **Opportunity.sql** has a complex amount parsing logic that might not work correctly. The European format detection needs to be fixed. Let me review:
   - "60.702,05" should become 60702.05
   - My current regex `(^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$)` would match this.
   - Then I'm doing `REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g')` which gives "60702,05", then splitting by comma to get parts... but my logic multiplies first part by 10 and divides last part by 10, which is wrong.

Let me fix the amount parsing. For European format "60.702,05":
- Remove dots: "60702,05"
- Split by comma: ["60702", "05"]
- Result should be 60702.05

The correct approach: `REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, '[\.]', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION` but wait that would give "60702,05" which can't cast to double precision directly.

Better approach: 
1. If pattern matches European format (contains `,` and `\.`): remove dots first, then replace comma with period
2. Otherwise just strip non-numeric characters except - and .

Let me rewrite the amount logic more carefully.

Also, for Opportunity StageName mapping, I added some German translations like 'angebot/preisanfrage' which may not exist in the data but won't hurt. The main issue is the Amount column parsing.

Let me fix all three files: Account.sql, Opportunity.sql, and potentially Contact.sql (though that looks OK).

Let me also double-check the Customer_Tier__c mapping for Account - looking at sample values:
- BRONZE, Bronze, bronze → Bronze ✓
- GOLD, Gold, gold → Gold ✓
- SILBER, Silver, silver → Silver (but SILBER is German for Silver, and there's no "Silber" in the target enum... wait, the target says Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum. So SILBER → Silver)
- Platin, Platinum, platinum → Platinum ✓

My original logic had:
```sql
WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('bronze', 'silber') THEN 'Bronze'  -- WRONG: silber should be Silver
WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('gold',) THEN 'Gold'  -- SYNTAX ERROR
WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('silver', 'silber') THEN 'Silver'  -- duplicate silber, would never reach this due to earlier match
WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('platinum', 'platin') THEN 'Platinum'
```

I need to fix:
1. Remove `silber` from Bronze case
2. Fix the syntax error in Gold case
3. Make sure all German terms are mapped correctly

Let me rewrite all affected files properly.

Also, I notice that the Contact model has `COALESCE(lastname, 'Unknown') AS "LastName"` - but the target says LastName is NOT NULL. However looking at the source data sample, lastname values are all present in the sample, so this COALESCE is defensive. That's fine.

Let me write corrected files now.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content="I can see several bugs in my models. Let me fix them:

2. **Opportunity.sql**: Amount parsing logic for European format is incorrect - the split/multiply approach is wrong.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 48, 0, 377057, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL

SELECT
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    firstname AS "FirstName",
    COALESCE(lastname, 'Unknown') AS "LastName",
    NULLIF(email, '') AS "Email",
    phone AS "Phone",
    title AS "Title",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(role__c, ''))) IN ('decision maker', 'entscheider') THEN 'Decision Maker'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(role__c, ''))) IN ('end user', 'endanwender') THEN 'End User'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(role__c, ''))) IN ('technical contact', 'technischer ansprechpartner', 'techniker') THEN 'Technical Contact'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(role__c, ''))) IN ('executive sponsor', 'sponsor') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Role__c",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('de', 'deutsch') THEN 'DE'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('en', 'english', 'englisch') THEN 'EN'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('fr', 'french', 'französisch') THEN 'FR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('es', 'spanish', 'spanisch') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('it', 'italian', 'italienisch') THEN 'IT'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'contact') }}

WITH cleaned_amounts AS (
    SELECT
        *,
        CASE
            WHEN amount IS NULL OR TRIM(amount) = '' THEN NULL
            ELSE
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(amount), E'^[\\$€£]+', '', 'g'),
                        E'^(?:Euro|Dollar|EUR|USD|GBP|CHF)\\s+', '', 'gi'
                    ),
                    E'[\\s]', ''
                )
        END AS raw_amount
    FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
)
SELECT
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preisanfrage') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYY-MM-DD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '0' THEN 0.0
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$' THEN
            (REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION * 10 +
            (SPLIT_PART(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'[.]', '', 'g'), ',', -1))::DOUBLE PRECISION / 10
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN
            (raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE
            CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', '$', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM cleaned_amounts

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The Account.sql was rewritten but I still need to fix the Opportunity.sql for the amount parsing logic. The issue is that European format amounts like "60.702,05" should become 60702.05. Let me think about the correct approach:

For "60.702,05":
1. First strip currency prefixes/symbols
2. If it matches European pattern (digits.digits,digits), remove dots and replace comma with period → "60702.05" → cast to double precision

For regular amounts like "-120228.71":
1. Strip currency prefixes
2. Direct cast to double precision

For "EUR 11833.1":
1. Strip "EUR " prefix → "11833.1"
2. Cast to double precision

My previous logic was trying to use SPLIT_PART which is wrong. Let me fix it:

```sql
CASE
    WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' THEN NULL
    WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\-?\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})+,\d+$' THEN
        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
    ELSE
        CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
END
```

This should work:
- "60.702,05" matches the European pattern → remove dots → "60702,05" → replace comma with period → "60702.05" → 60702.05 ✓
- "-120228.71" doesn't match → direct cast → -120228.71 ✓

Let me also handle the case where raw_amount is '0' or just whitespace after stripping - should return NULL or 0? Let me check if there are any '0' amounts... Looking at earlier data, there was an amount of "0" which probably means $0 deal. I'll convert empty/zero to 0.

Also, I need to re-examine the regex more carefully. The pattern `^\-?\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})+,\d+$` would match:
- "60.702,05" ✓ (two digit groups before decimal, three after)
- But what about "1.234,56"? Yes, that matches too.

Actually wait, what if an amount like "1234,56" (no thousand separator dots)? That would NOT match my pattern. Let me think... If it has a comma but no dots, it could be European decimal:
- "1234,56" → replace comma with period → 1234.56

Let me update the logic to handle this case too:

```sql
CASE
    WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' THEN NULL
    -- European format with thousand-separator dots: "60.702,05"
    WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\-?\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})+,\d+$' THEN
        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
    -- European format without thousand separators: "1234,56"
    WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\-?\d+,\d+$' AND raw_amount !~ '\.' THEN
        REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
    ELSE
        CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
END
```

This is better. Let me rewrite the Opportunity.sql with this fix and also make sure all other logic is correct.

Also I should double-check my StageName mappings are complete and correct based on the sample data I've seen.

Let me write the corrected files now.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"content": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\n\nSELECT\n    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS \"Id\",\n    COALESCE(name, 'Unknown') AS \"Name\",\n    erp_number__c AS \"ERP_Number__c\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('bronze',) THEN 'Bronze'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('gold',) THEN 'Gold'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('silver', 'silber') THEN 'Silver'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('platinum', 'platin') THEN 'Platinum'\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Customer_Tier__c\",\n    region__c AS \"Region__c\",\n    industry AS \"Industry\",\n    website AS \"Website\",\n    billingcity AS \"BillingCity\",\n    billingcountry AS \"BillingCountry\",\n    legacy_customer_id__c AS \"Legacy_Customer_ID__c\",\n    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}\n", "path": "models/Account.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_upv332a5')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=17485, output_tokens=1167), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 54, 53, 475103, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 54, 53, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-30b2befa-c666-44d1-b237-a15cfcbfafe7', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 48, 0, 377057, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL

- `Region__c` text

## Contact
Columns:

- `LastName` text NOT NULL

- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 48, 0, 377067, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 48, 0, 377421, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4dc9-8577-71cc-8b72-6da8cc9f484e', conversation_id='019f4dc9-8577-71cc-8b72-6da7819bd6fc')

**Account (fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account):**

- `id`: CUST-1001 style (Salesforce-style Account Id)

- `role__c` values: Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor, "Entscheider", "Sponsor", "Technischer Ansprechpartner", etc.
  - Map to: Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor
- `preferred_language__c` values: DE, Deutsch, EN, Englisch, English, FR, Französisch, French, German, etc.
  - Map to: DE, EN, FR, ES, IT

**Opportunity (fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity):**
- `stagename`: closed won, LOST, Prospecting, Qualification, "In Prüfung", "LOST", etc.
  - Map to: Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost
- `closedate`: Multiple formats: DD.MM.YYYY, MM/DD/YYYY, YYYYMMDD, YYYY-MM-DD
- `amount`: Various formats including European (150.721,39), "EUR 11833.1", "$" for currency

**Project__c (fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c):**
- `project_status__c`: Active, "on hold", "Planung", "Aktiv", "Abgeschlossen", etc.
  - Map to: Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled

**Installed_Asset__c (fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c):**

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preisanfrage') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYY-MM-DD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '0' THEN 0.0
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$' THEN
            (REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION * 10 +
            (SPLIT_PART(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'[.]', '', 'g'), ',', -1))::DOUBLE PRECISION / 10
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN
            (raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE
            CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', '$', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM cleaned_amounts

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 411861, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/Account.sql

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 411861, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit.   compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/Opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 411871, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 412178, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b90a822ee5a4', conversation_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b909cafd3ca5')

ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(name, 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    erp_number__c AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('bronze',) THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('gold',) THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('silver', 'silber') THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('platinum', 'platin') THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    region__c AS "Region__c",
    industry AS "Industry",
    website AS "Website",
    billingcity AS "BillingCity",
    billingcountry AS "BillingCountry",
    legacy_customer_id__c AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}
', tool_call_id='call_9l2igutn', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 41, 531514, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

WITH cleaned_amounts AS (
    SELECT
        *,
        CASE
            WHEN amount IS NULL OR TRIM(amount) = '' THEN NULL
            ELSE
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(amount), E'^[\\$€£]+', '', 'g'),
                        E'^(?:Euro|Dollar|EUR|USD|GBP|CHF)\\s+', '', 'gi'
                    ),
                    E'[\\s]', ''
                )
        END AS raw_amount
    FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
)
SELECT
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preisanfrage') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) = 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYY-MM-DD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '0' THEN 0.0
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$' THEN
            (REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION * 10 +
            (SPLIT_PART(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'[.]', '', 'g'), ',', -1))::DOUBLE PRECISION / 10
        WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN
            (raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE
            CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', '$', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM cleaned_amounts
', tool_call_id='call_luja4avo', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 41, 532012, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 41, 532460, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b90a822ee5a4', conversation_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b909cafd3ca5')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Let me analyze both issues:

1. **Account model** - The syntax error is from `IN ('bronze',)` — there's a trailing comma inside the parentheses which is invalid SQL. I need to remove those trailing commas in all the IN clauses.

2. **Opportunity model** - The error "invalid input syntax for type double precision: ''" means an empty string is being cast to DOUBLE PRECISION somewhere. Looking at the CTE and the final SELECT, the issue might be in the European amount parsing or the fallback `CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)`. If `raw_amount` is an empty string after cleaning, the earlier NULL check should catch it... but wait, let me trace through:

In the CTE, `raw_amount` is cleaned by stripping currency symbols and text. Then in the final SELECT:
- First CASE checks if `raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = ''` — returns 0.0
- Second CASE matches European format with dots as thousands separator
- Third CASE catches `^\d+,\d+$` pattern (simple comma decimal without thousand separators) — this does `(raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION` directly
- ELSE does `CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)`

The issue might be that after all the cleaning, some values that look like numbers but are actually just empty or whitespace get through. Actually wait — the issue could be that after REGEXP_REPLACE removes currency prefixes and text, if the amount was something like "$" alone, it becomes an empty string "". But the first CASE should catch `TRIM(raw_amount) = ''`.

Hmm, let me think again. The error says "invalid input syntax for type double precision: """. This is coming from a CAST or ::DOUBLE PRECISION operation on an empty string.

Wait - looking more carefully at the second pattern:
```sql
WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$' THEN
     (REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION * 10 +
     (SPLIT_PART(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'[.]', '', 'g'), ',', -1))::DOUBLE PRECISION / 10
```

After `REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g')`, the dots are removed, so `1.234,56` becomes `1234,56`. Then it tries to cast that to DOUBLE PRECISION directly, which would fail because of the comma!

The logic seems confused - it's trying to handle European numbers but doing it wrong. Let me fix this properly:

For European format like `1.234,56`:
1. Remove thousand-separator dots: `REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g')` → `1234,56`
2. Replace comma with dot: `REPLACE(..., ',', '.')` → `1234.56`
3. Cast to DOUBLE PRECISION

Or simpler approach - just do the replacement chain properly.

Let me also handle the simpler European case where there's no thousand separator dots:
- Pattern `^\d+,\d+$` like `1234,56`: Replace comma with dot, then cast.

For the simple non-European case (no commas):
- Just strip and cast directly.

Let me also look at whether empty strings could slip through. If the original amount was NULL or empty string, after REGEXP_REPLACE it stays NULL or empty. The first CASE should handle both cases... but wait, what if raw_amount is an empty string? `TRIM('') = ''` should be true. Unless there's a whitespace-only value?

Actually, I think the real issue might be that some amounts are just spaces or have weird characters that slip through all the cleaning and end up as something like "  " which TRIM becomes "" but the check might not catch it properly... or maybe there are other edge cases.

Let me rewrite both models to fix these issues:

For **Account**:
- Fix the trailing commas in IN clauses: `IN ('bronze',)` → `IN ('bronze')`

For **Opportunity**:
- Fix the European amount parsing logic
- Add better guards against empty strings before casting', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content="I see both issues:

1. **Account**: Trailing commas in `IN ('bronze',)` — invalid SQL syntax. Need to remove those.
2. **Opportunity**: The European number parsing has a bug — after removing dots, the result still contains a comma which fails `::DOUBLE PRECISION`. Also need tighter guards before any cast.

Let me fix both models now."), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/Account.sql", "content": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\n\nSELECT\n    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS \"Id\",\n    COALESCE(name, 'Unknown') AS \"Name\",\n    erp_number__c AS \"ERP_Number__c\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('bronze') THEN 'Bronze'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('gold') THEN 'Gold'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('silver', 'silber') THEN 'Silver'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('platinum', 'platin') THEN 'Platinum'\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Customer_Tier__c\",\n    region__c AS \"Region__c\",\n    industry AS \"Industry\",\n    website AS \"Website\",\n    billingcity AS \"BillingCity\",\n    billingcountry AS \"BillingCountry\",\n    legacy_customer_id__c AS \"Legacy_Customer_ID__c\",\n    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n     0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}"}', tool_call_id='call_5n4qlgxm')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=3016, output_tokens=1304), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 56, 15, 16474, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 56, 15, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-188d7cf3-90c0-41a2-b83c-d87e34072dcb', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 411861, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

Errors:
overall_status: partial
- Account: Database Error in model Account (models/Account.sql)
  syntax error at or near ")"
  LINE 19: ...  WHEN LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('bronze',) THEN 'Br...
                                                                ^
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/Account.sql
- Opportunity: Database Error in model Opportunity (models/Opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: ""
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/Opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 411871, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 27, 412178, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b90a822ee5a4', conversation_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b909cafd3ca5')

Let me first read the current models to understand what's wrong and fix them.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content="I'll start by reading both failing models to understand the issues."), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/Account.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_9l2igutn'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='read_file', args='{"path": "models/Opportunity.sql"}', tool_call_id='call_luja4avo')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=1650, output_tokens=182), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 41, 529154, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 55, 41, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-442e0ba5-06e8-4554-b55f-5e3621fa6708', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b90a822ee5a4', conversation_id='019f4dd0-57b2-70c9-983b-b909cafd3ca5')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The Account model looks good - the trailing commas are removed. Now I need to fix the Opportunity model.

The issues in the Opportunity model's Amount calculation:

1. **European number parsing bug**: The pattern `^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+$` matches numbers like `1.234,56`. But then:
   - `(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION * 10` — after removing dots, we get `1234,56`, which cannot be cast to DOUBLE PRECISION because of the comma.
   
   The logic seems confused. Let me think about what the correct approach should be:
   - European format: `1.234,56` means 1234.56
   - We need to: remove dots (thousand separators), replace comma with dot → `1234.56`, then cast
   
   Simplified: `REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION`

2. **Simple European format** (`^\d+,\d+$` like `1234,56`): The code does `(raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION` which fails because of the comma. Fix: replace comma with dot first.

3. **The else branch** `CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` could receive values that slip through all cleaning. Need to add a guard.

Let me also consider what the original data might look like. The REGEXP_REPLACE in the CTE strips `$€£` prefixes, text prefixes like "Euro 1234", and whitespace. But if the amount is something weird like just a currency symbol "$" or empty after cleaning, the first CASE should catch it with `TRIM(raw_amount) = ''`.

But wait - what about values that are not caught by the regex patterns but aren't empty either? For example:
- If raw_amount ends up as "abc" after cleaning (which shouldn't happen normally), it would fall through to the ELSE branch and fail to cast.

Let me also check — the first CASE handles `raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '0'`. But what about strings that are purely whitespace? `TRIM('   ') = ''` should be true, so that's handled.

Actually, I think there might be another issue. What if an amount is like `$` (just a dollar sign with no number)? After REGEXP_REPLACE strips the `$`, we get an empty string `''`. The first CASE catches `TRIM(raw_amount) = ''`, so this should return 0.0.

Hmm, but what if there's some Unicode whitespace that TRIM doesn't handle? Or what about values like `   $1.234,56   ` where the cleaning leaves something weird?

Let me focus on the obvious bugs and add a safety net in the ELSE branch:

For the Amount field:
```sql
CASE
    WHEN raw_amount IS NULL OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '' OR TRIM(raw_amount) = '0' THEN 0.0
    WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d{1,3}(\.\d{3})*,\d+(\.\d+)?$' THEN
        -- European format with thousand-separator dots: "1.234,56"
        -- Remove dots, replace comma with dot, cast
        (REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(raw_amount, E'\.', '', 'g'), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION)
    WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN
        -- Simple European decimal: "1234,56"
        (REPLACE(raw_amount, ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION)
    WHEN raw_amount ~ '^\d+(\.\d+)?$' THEN
        -- Standard decimal: "1234.56"
        (CAST(raw_amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION))
    ELSE 0.0
END AS "Amount"
```

This is cleaner and handles the European formats properly by replacing comma with dot before casting.

Let me also remove the unnecessary multiplication/division logic that was there before — it was broken anyway. The simple `REPLACE(..., ',', '.')` approach works for all European cases.

Now let me write this fix.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content="Account is fixed. Now I see the Opportunity bugs clearly:

1. **European number parsing**: After removing dots with `REGEXP_REPLACE`, the result still has a comma → `::DOUBLE PRECISION` fails. Need `REPLACE(..., ',', '.')` before casting.
2. **Simple European format** (`^\d+,\d+$`): Same issue — `(raw_amount)::DOUBLE PRECISION` on a value with a comma will fail.

Let me fix the Opportunity model:")], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=5494, output_tokens=1042), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 57, 0, 169538, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'stop', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 57, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-016e2313-e04d-496c-b8c4-01bcadc6e9bc', finish_reason='stop')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 20, 57, 15, 179034, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.
